text.title: django-constants Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for django-constants. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: All CharField and IntegerField with choices MUST use TextChoices or IntegerChoices subclasses. The field definition uses choices=ClassName.choices and default=ClassName.VALUE. Raw strings in choices= are forbidden.
rule.rationale: TextChoices integrates with Django's migration framework, provides .choices for field definitions, .values for filtering, and .labels for display. Raw string choices scatter values across the codebase and cannot be refactored with rename tools.

rule.statement: Each app MUST have a constants.py containing all TextChoices/IntegerChoices classes and named numeric limits for that domain. Limits MUST be UPPER_SNAKE_CASE. No numeric literals or string literals for business constraints anywhere in services, selectors, or models — always import from constants.py.
rule.rationale: Centralizing limits makes them visible, documented, and changeable in one place. A developer changing MAX_ORDERS_PER_USER from 50 to 100 edits one line. Without named constants, they must grep for 50 across the entire codebase and hope no other field has that value.

rule.statement: Use TextChoices for string status fields, not raw str tuples. Access enum values as ClassName.VALUE not as string literals. Compare enum values to each other (status == OrderStatus.PENDING) not to strings (status == "pending").
rule.rationale: Comparing against enum values is refactoring-safe and IDE-completable. Comparing against string literals creates invisible dependencies on exact string values that are invisible to rename tools. TextChoices.VALUE is also easier to discover via autocomplete.
